---
title: "Testing and README"
description: "Protect capstone behavior with tests and explain the project clearly."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Protect capstone behavior with tests and explain the project clearly.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one clear explanation.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Testing and README** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 2 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- **1. Testing Your Project** — Add practical tests that protect the main behavior of your capstone.
- **2. Writing a README** — Explain what your project does, how to run it, and how to understand the results.

## 1. Testing Your Project

Add practical tests that protect the main behavior of your capstone.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Tests are guardrails on a bridge: they do not move the project forward, but they keep it
from falling.
:::

### What this means

Project tests capture examples of correct behavior so changes can be made safely.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
def normalize_name(name):
    return name.strip().title()

assert normalize_name(" ada ") == "Ada"
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `def normalize_name(name):` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `return name.strip().title()` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
3. `assert normalize_name(" ada ") == "Ada"` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Do not postpone all tests until the end. Add tests when behavior becomes important.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "capstone-testing-your-project",
  "title": "Testing Your Project checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Testing Your Project?",
      "options": [
        "Project tests capture examples of correct behavior so changes can be made safely.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Project tests capture examples of correct behavior so changes can be made safely."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Tests are guardrails on a bridge: they do not move the project forward, but they keep it from falling."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 2. Writing a README

Explain what your project does, how to run it, and how to understand the results.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A README is a museum label: it gives enough context for visitors to appreciate what they
are seeing.
:::

### What this means

A README is the front door to your project for future you and other people.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```markdown
# Project Title

### What it does
Short explanation.

### How to run
`python main.py`
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `# Project Title` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `## What it does` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
3. `Short explanation.` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
4. `## How to run` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
5. ``python main.py`` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
A README that only says the title does not help someone run or evaluate your work.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "capstone-writing-a-readme",
  "title": "Writing a README checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Writing a README?",
      "options": [
        "A README is the front door to your project for future you and other people.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A README is the front door to your project for future you and other people."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A README is a museum label: it gives enough context for visitors to appreciate what they are seeing."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
first example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Testing Your Project**: Project tests capture examples of correct behavior so changes can be made safely.
- **Writing a README**: A README is the front door to your project for future you and other people.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python packaging user guide: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/
- The Turing Way reproducible research: https://book.the-turing-way.org/reproducible-research/reproducible-research
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
